facts_disclosed: Thinking about hiring an apprentice to help with the workload

turns.content: alright so I need to think something through and youre good for bouncing ideas off. its about the business
Happy to help you think it through. What's going on with the business?

so basically the amount of work coming in is mad right now. like properly mad. Im turning down jobs cos I physically cant get to them all. had to say no to three customers last week and thats money walking out the door

Turning down work is a good problem to have in one sense -- it means the demand is there and people want to hire you. But you're right, three refusals in a week is real revenue you're leaving on the table. Are you thinking about ways to increase your capacity?

yeah thats exactly it. ive been mulling over taking on an apprentice. someone I can train up properly you know. give them the right habits from day one. I came up doing it the hard way and I reckon I could pass on a lot of what ive learned

An apprentice could be a really smart move. You get an extra pair of hands to help with the workload, and over time they develop into a skilled plumber who knows your standards. What's drawing you to an apprentice specifically rather than hiring someone already qualified?

cos if I hire someone qualified theyve already got their own habits and ways of doing things. with an apprentice I can shape them from scratch. teach them the right way to do things not just the fast way. theres something proper satisfying about that too innit like giving someone the chance I had to fight for

That's a really great way to look at it. There's genuine value in mentoring someone from the ground up -- they learn your methods, your quality standards, and your work ethic. And you're right that it's a chance to give someone an opportunity. Have you looked into the practical side yet? Things like the apprenticeship frameworks, what the costs look like, how the training college side works?

not properly no. I know theres NVQ level 2 and 3 in plumbing and they go to college one day a week or something like that. the rest of the time theyre with me on site learning. I think the government covers some of the training costs but im not sure about the details

You've got the right general picture. For plumbing apprenticeships in England, the typical route is a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship standard. The apprentice splits time between on-the-job training with you and off-the-job learning, usually at a college or training provider. 

On funding: if the apprentice is 16-18, the government typically covers 100% of the training costs. For 19 and over, you'd usually co-invest -- the government covers 95% and you pay 5%. Since you're a small business, you might also be eligible for additional incentive payments.

The main costs to you would be the apprentice's wages (at least the apprentice minimum wage, though many employers pay more to attract good candidates), employer's liability insurance, and any tools or equipment they need. Would you want me to break down the financial side in more detail?
yeah the money side is what I need to get my head around. wages I get, thats fine. but the insurance bit and all the employer stuff thats where it gets complicated for a sole trader like me

Good instinct to focus there -- that's where the hidden costs live. As a sole trader taking on an apprentice, you'd need to:

1. **Employer's liability insurance** -- legally required, covers the apprentice if they're injured at work. This is separate from your existing public liability insurance. Costs vary but typically a few hundred to a few thousand pounds a year depending on the trade and provider.

2. **PAYE registration** -- you'd need to register as an employer with HMRC and run payroll, handling tax and National Insurance contributions.

3. **Workplace pension** -- auto-enrolment rules apply even with one employee.

4. **Tools and PPE** -- you'd need to provide safety equipment and possibly basic tools.

5. **Reduced productivity initially** -- the apprentice will slow you down at first because you're teaching, not just working. That evens out over time as they become more useful.

The biggest question is whether the extra revenue from taking on more jobs outweighs these costs. Do you have a rough idea of how much work you're turning away in a typical month?

probably losing a grand or two a month in turned away work if im honest. some months more. the insurance and PAYE stuff sounds manageable but id need to sit down with the numbers properly. Simran would probably sort the payroll side shes dead good with that stuff

If you're turning away one to two thousand a month, that's twelve to twenty-four thousand a year in potential revenue -- more than enough to cover an apprentice's wages and the associated costs, especially after the first year when they start being genuinely productive. Having Simran handle the payroll side would be a massive advantage since she clearly has a head for that kind of thing.

A good next step would be to contact your local college or training provider to understand the apprenticeship framework, and get a quote on employer's liability insurance so you have real numbers to work with. You could also look at the National Apprenticeship Service website -- they have a tool that walks employers through the whole process.

yeah I think thats the move. get the actual numbers then make the call. no point guessing about it. I just like the idea of it you know. someone coming in green and leaving properly skilled. thats what happened to me, someone gave me a shot and it changed everything

That's a powerful motivation and honestly it's what makes the best mentors -- people who remember what it was like to be given that first chance. If you do go ahead with it, that attitude is going to make you a brilliant employer to learn from. Get those numbers together, have a proper look at the insurance costs, and you'll be able to make a clear-headed decision.
sorted mate. ill look into it this week and chat to the college. cheers for laying it all out like that proper helpful
Glad I could help you think it through. It sounds like a solid opportunity if the numbers work out. Good luck with the research, and let me know how it goes with the college.
